sentences.text: Yes, of course.
The papers, I got through illegal ways.
The first paper I got to a friend which worked together with our people-- with this small group of underground people on the Polish side.
She was Anna Wonchalska-- her name.
She was a socialist but she-- Michael Klepfisz, who was the one who introduced me to her, brought me over to her house.
And little by little, she likes me and she told me, you know-- Vladka-- what?
You need papers to have an original Kin Card-- the identification paper from-- by-- made officially by the German administration.
And I had a little daughter, she told me, who was two years old.
She died.
So I will take the birth certificate out from this small town from my daughter and you will become my daughter.
And so I became her daughter-- Anna Wonchalska's daughter.
This was my first identification card.
The real identification card, which I didn't have for too long.
Because always, somebody was blackmailing and it was always a danger.
If you have too long and somebody else got arrested, you are afraid that the person can give you out.
But Anna Wonchalska was really one of the righteous Polish women.
And she was like a mother.
And once, on the-- during the Uprising, when I was on the Polish side, I need some help.
And always when I need help, I turned to Anna Wonchalska.
She had contact with Polish people.
And she was helping us-- the Jewish-- the few Jewish underground workers on the Polish side, which belonged to the fighters' organization.
When Kashak Rotheiser and Zalman Friedrich came out from the ghetto looking for help to save a few ghetto fighters, we turned, as usually, to Anna Wonchalska for some contacts.
And at that time, they were also instrumental contacting some people who have trucks.
And the people who had the trucks didn't know for what they are really going-- what kind of job and what kind of tasks they are going to-- to do.
But they were hired as smugglers and they understood this way.
They were brought to the place where Kashak knew that the group is going to wait.
And it was very early in the morning.
Kashak Rotheiser with the two people, of course, under a gun because they got frightened.
They opened-- the opening from the sewers and over 60 people of ghetto fighters came out terrible disheveled.
And on the truck, one was in a German uniform staying on guard on the truck.
The other were hidden under blankets.
And they went out from this street to a small place under-- not far from Warsaw.
The other group, which remained and was waiting for the other truck, was not able to get out anymore.
Germans came and it was a shooting.
And one of mine co-workers was, at that time, also killed.
This was the only, really, group at that particular time after-- it was not after the Uprising, but at the end of the Uprising, which was able to get out from the Warsaw ghetto.
In the woods, they were not able to be too long.
The surrounding Polish population, if they will discover who they are, they will give them out.
And it was much more difficult to hide and to find a hiding place in Warsaw.
Because the Germans-- they had special orders that those who were hiding Jews-- they will be killed, as well as the Jews.
And the whole house will be killed.
Nevertheless, two people were smuggled out from the ghetto and one of them was Abraham Blum, one of the leaders.
And he didn't have a place where to go and he was located in mine apartment for the time being.
We were looking for places-- how to help the ghetto.
But it was very difficult-- almost impossible.
And my feeling was, at that time, that everything really comes to an end.
They tell to me that we have to go and to print some special poster from the Polish underground from the-- that the Jews are fighting for their freedom, for the ghetto, and from the Poles.
And the printing shop-- the illegal Polish printing shop-- was in [NON-ENGLISH].
It was also on outskirt of Warsaw.
I took this-- this-- writing to the shop over there, but I have to go through-- alongside the Polish side of the ghetto wall and there were people staying there.
And the smoke was so dark, and flames from the ghetto.
And I heard how people were talking.
Oh, take a look.
The Jews are frying.
Or take a look, it's good that they came already to an end, that Warsaw will be liberated from Jews.
It was a dangerous piece of walking distance to go.
But nevertheless, I made it and I came back with the posters which has to be put up at night on the houses.
When I brought to back the posters, two evenings later, in mine house with Abraham Blum, Gestapo-- a Gestapo person and a civilian came up and they closed the apartment.
They took out everything and they found the Jews at Abraham.
I didn't admit that I am Jewish.
They took-- whatever it was valuable, they took.
And they told me that-- to remain there.
You are being closed and the Gestapo will come in a few minutes to take you out.
Abraham and I remained in the apartment-- in mine apartment-- and we had how the lock on the other side is closing.
We didn't know what to do.
We waited half an hour, an hour.
Nobody showed up.
And finally, we start talking and I said to Abraham, what do we do with the remaining posters which we have?
We have to disregard them.
We have, somehow, not-- if anybody comes up, they should not find that we belong to a illegal organization.
Then it will be even worse than being just the Jews because they will torture us.
I started to disregard the papers-- to burn, to flush.
And when we did it, we were waiting.
Nobody came up.
And then Abraham Blum told me, you know, we have to get out of here.
But this is on the floor-- fourth floor.
How could we get out from the fourth floor?
So we took the sheet and we put them together.
And we fastened it to the radiator.
And then it was, who will go first?
And I said to Abraham, listen, you have a wife.
But while lowering himself down, he said, you know what?
I will bind you a little bit that you couldn't do anything if something happened.
That you couldn't do and prevent this.
And while he bound me and he lowered hisself, suddenly, I heard a very big noise.
He fall down.
I don't know from how high he fall down.
But he fall down and he woke up the whole building and police came up together with him.
And they took us both to the-- he was able to walk.
They took us both to the police station, provided that in the morning, the Gestapo-- the Gestapo was not there, but the Polish police.
We went to the police station and over there, I was thinking, what do I do now?
How can I notify our people not to come to mine apartment?
Because it will be a danger not only for us, but also for the other.
It was in the middle of the night and I didn't come up with any idea.
I was put in a cell where the prostitutes were and he was taken to the men's side.
In the morning, around 7:00 or 7:30, one of the policemen was changing the guard.
And he is going home.
And I found in mine pocket, I found a few zlotys and I said to him, maybe you can notify my mother.
And my mother was Anna Wonchalska.
Just notify my mother that I am here and it's a misunderstanding that they found a Jew in my-- I didn't say that I am Jewish-- in mine apartment.
And please do it.
I was not sure that he is going to do it.
But it turned out, he did.
Maybe he thought that I belonged to the underground.
Maybe it was one of the Polish policemen who had a little bit of heart.
After all, I was a young girl at that time.
And they took away Abraham Blum to the Gestapo, telling me that I am going next from the Polish police station.
And I was sure that this is the end.
How I felt at that time-- I didn't feel anything.
All kinds of thoughts went through.
I was not afraid.
I knew, it is the end.
A half an hour later, after-- or an hour later-- after Abraham was taken away, the door opened, and one of Anna Wonchalska's nephew came in and he told me, don't be afraid.
We bribed the captain from the police station.
Well, it turned out that the policeman gave my note to Anna Wonchalska.
She knew one off our illegal meeting points.
She went there and she started screaming, they have to do everything to take me out.
And for 10,000 zlotys, it can be done because she is still in the police-- in the Polish police prison.
And they-- somehow, they get together the amount of money very fast and they bribe the captain.
And this nephew took me out from the Polish police station.
And this is how I survived and Abraham Blum was taken.
This was one time when, really, I don't know how to thank Anna Wonchalska because she really rescued my life.
This was a remarkable righteous person-- remarkable.
On the Polish side, during the Uprising, we felt-- the few Jewish underground workers-- that, really, things come to an end.
The ghetto, the hiding places will-- that maybe we will be not able, even, to survive.
We had the list.
Nevertheless, after the Uprising, a short while later, again the underground summoned me to start looking for hiding places and to start to contact other camps.
And to do the utmost to give them-- to send them some illegal papers, some illegal identification cards.
To rescue as many as we are able.
So mine task was not Warsaw anymore so much-- to contact other camps.
And I was the one who assumed new identification card.
And I went to Radom camp.
I went to [NON-ENGLISH] camp.
I went to a partisan group not far from [NON-ENGLISH].
From the [NON-ENGLISH] partisan group.
When I came to them, it was a group from [NON-ENGLISH].
They were hidden in a-- in a small shack full of-- high-- full of dirt.
They didn't believe when I came that I am from another political group.
Who send it?
And I told them that [INAUDIBLE] Zuckerman sent me.
The one who came out six days before the Uprising on the Polish side to prepare more ammunition.
But that much, it was-- he was able to do.
But he had contact that in certain places of young people from [NON-ENGLISH] are hiding.
They also belong to the fighters' organization in this-- in the other city and they don't have any contact.
And we have to help them.
And when I came, they told me that it was the last time that they have money and if I would not have come and brought them some money, they will not be able to remain in the hiding place.
So I was there.
I slept over with them.
And it was, to me, part of myself.
Young people-- I never saw them in my life.
But Jews-- people who survive.
And then they also-- the only request they have-- don't forget us.
Remember.
They gave me some pictures to prepare them for identification cards in case they will have to go out.
And I went a few times there.
And one time when I came to the same place, nobody was there.
It turned out it was after a long while.
We didn't have any money.
It was impossible to go on trains.
And I very often went on trains as a smuggler without any special identification or permission.
But nevertheless, I went.
It was like a commitment-- an inner commitment to go.
And when I came, nobody was there.
And I asked the person, where are they?
What happened?
She was not able to feed them and they have to leave because the whole village start talking that she's hiding Jews.
And they went to the woods-- to the forest.
But, she said, if you will go in this and this direction to the forest, the nearest house is the-- the person who is guarding there.
Ask them if they saw a group.
And I went there.
It was everything to go on foot.
It was not going by train, not going by car.
And when I came there, the man told me, yes, that there are some Jews who are coming at night.
And maybe if you will sleep over, you will be able to meet them.
I slept over.
And it's luck again.
At the same night, they was knocking at the door.
At the window, not at the door.
And they recognized me.
And it turned out that of the 11 who were hiding, six perished already and they were killed by Polish partisans, not by Jews, not by Germans, not by just smugglers or blackmailers.
But by partisan, who were fighting the same German, the same enemy.
They killed the Jews.
Why?
Because they were anti-Semite.
Because they couldn't understand that other partisans have also right to survive.
Not they couldn't understand-- they couldn't feel.
And the only-- the only way how to protect them was to ask a letter from the leadership of the Polish partisan-- of the higher echelon, that maybe this will help.
Later on in Israel, two of the people from this group, I met.
They survived.
With the money, they came back to the old hiding place and the five survived.
And two, I met later in Israel.
So my task, after the Uprising, was to contact camps, to contact people in hiding, and what was only able to help them to survive.
Nobody has contact, during the Uprising, with the ghetto.
You read sometimes in the Polish papers or books about contacts.
We, on the Polish side, the leadership, the few are were in contact-- one of our leaders was [NON-ENGLISH]-- he's dead already-- and he was in contact with the Polish underground.
We didn't have any direct contact, although some claim that in this and this part, some group of [NON-ENGLISH]-- of the liberal-- of the leftist, was helping them.
We didn't know at that time about this, at all.
They were alone.
The ghetto was absolutely alone, forsaken.
And even today, when I think and I look back, history is being made-- by Poles or by others.
Our Jewish people were absolutely isolated and by themself.
And I think that we have to remember this because history, sometimes, is being made by political groups.
And what is good for them and what the image they would like to be seen in history, they write it down.
But the reality was different.
We were forsaken.
And I think that in the future, and even today, history has to inscribe the truthful fact how it was really.
We were even forsaken by the allies-- by the outside world.
By the so-called free world.
We turned to them.
Later on, I was in the Polish Uprising.
And together with the Polish people as a Polish girl, I was witnessing how the planes came from England-- from London, from other places-- and they were dropping ammunition.
They were dropping medication.
But when the Warsaw ghetto Uprising took place, our skies were empty.
We didn't have any help for anybody.
The ghetto went up in flame.
And even the free world didn't do anything.
I remember we write about Artur Zygelbojm later on that in London, he commit suicide, May 8.
His wife and his child perished in the Warsaw ghetto.
And I was connected with this case.
I want to mention it.
I found a place for the little boy.
He was eight years old-- Artur Zygelbojm's boy.
And the mother-- I described the place to the mother and I told her that it's a place only for the child.
It's not for you.
Maybe later on.
But right now, you have to decide.
And Abraham Blum, who was the one who was talking to her, too, said, you know, make the decision.
Give away the child.
We know that something is going to happen.
But her decision was not to separate.
She went through all the interpretations and he will perish if she is not there.
And she decided no.
And they both perished.
And Artur Zygelbojm committed suicide not because only of her.
He could not arouse the interest and the help of the outside world-- of the free outside world for the help of the Jews.
And this is also history, should remember.
Of course it was very difficult decisions to give away a child.
But at the same time, this was the only decision.
The two girls-- the Dunkels-- which I-- one is Dunkel.
She married.
She's in the States, which I helped to survive.
The mother gave away the child.
Both girls.
They were twins.
And she said that she will perish, by at least, maybe there's a chance for the two girls.
And of course, they were difficulties to hide them, to change places.
Somehow, they survived.
It's difficult to say that she did the right or wrong in this circumstances, of course.
But I just tell you how situations are.
I don't blame the mother who didn't [? park. ?] And I don't encourage mother who did-- I don't know how I would act.
I don't know.
But this is the situation, how it was.
You didn't know what to anticipate at that time.
I was in Warsaw when the Polish Uprising broke out.
It was in August, 1944.
I was together with the Polish people.
And in the first two days, three days, we saw the German army getting back and going back from the east to the west.
And we thought that it was the right time.
A day-- two, three-- and we will be liberated by the Russians.
But the Russians stop at the Vistula-- at the river-- and didn't go further.
And in meantime, the German occupied-- not-- didn't lose the occupation of Warsaw, but they remained in Warsaw.
And Uprising was a very bloody Uprising.
I tell you, I was part of the Uprising together with the Polish population, preparing things and-- and doing what-- what the responsible people asked to do.
But particular, in the Polish, as a Jew, I didn't have the contact of the [NON-ENGLISH].
But I was part of the Polish population-- as a Jew, but as a Pole.
And at that time, it-- we had a very difficult problem with Jews who were hiding in Polish apartments.
Because they have to get out of it and many didn't have where to go.
And many were cold and many were killed.
The Polish Uprising took by two months.
And then, all of Warsaw was evacuated.
And at that time, I and Ben were together and his parents were in a hiding place with Poles and they were in another place.
They were also evacuated.
They survived, but as Pole.
And we were also out of Warsaw and we survived in a small village-- in Grojec-- as Poles.
And his father survived as a person who cannot hear and cannot talk.
So his Polish was very bad and had the Jewish accent.
He looks good, but till he opened his mouth.
And if he opened his mouth, right away, you knew he was Jewish.
This was organized by the Polish underground, the Armia [NON-ENGLISH].
The Polish military underground.
So it was participation of the Armia Ludowa.
This is a smaller Polish group.
The more leftest group.
After the Uprising, when all-- after two months, the German evacuate all of Warsaw.
And it took till-- from September till January 17.
The new attack of the Russian liberated Warsaw.
So the Russian came in in January to Warsaw.
And this is when I came back to Warsaw.
